name: THREAT Matrix
full_name: Tactical Human Risk Enumeration and Adversary Taxonomy Matrix

tactics.name: Target Selection
tactics.notes: Who, what, and why this target. Precedes all reconnaissance. Actor evaluates candidate individuals against attack objectives — relationship to grievance, symbolic value, access feasibility, and vulnerability. The decision to focus on a specific named individual marks the transition from general grievance to targeted threat.
tactics.cpn_notes: Social media, LinkedIn, data brokers, and OSINT tools are primary sources for modern target selection. Digital footprint analysis can produce a detailed vulnerability assessment before any physical reconnaissance occurs.

tactics.name: Subject Profiling
tactics.notes: Sustained pattern-of-life intelligence collection on a specific named person. Actor builds a behavioral and spatial model of the subject: daily schedule, regular locations, social relationships, personal routines, and response patterns. This tactic is ongoing — it does not complete before Phase 2 begins.
tactics.cpn_notes: Digital profile — social media activity, location check-ins, fitness tracker data, public records aggregation — often precedes and supplements physical surveillance. Apps that publicly share routes or check-ins provide operationally precise location intelligence.

tactics.name: Environmental Survey
tactics.notes: Physical site assessment of locations associated with the subject — residence, workplace, vehicle routes, regular venues. Actor builds a spatial model of environments where the subject is present and vulnerable. Distinct from Subject Profiling (which focuses on the person); this tactic focuses on the locations.
tactics.cpn_notes: Satellite imagery, Google Maps Street View, live webcams, and publicly posted photos and videos of locations augment or replace physical walkthroughs. High-resolution imagery can provide actionable access and egress analysis before the actor visits a location in person.

tactics.name: Capability Acquisition
tactics.notes: Actor acquires the means to execute the planned action — weapons, tools, financing, false credentials, vehicles, communications equipment. Capability acquisition is bounded by the planned technique: the acquisition profile reveals the intended method.
tactics.cpn_notes: Dark web procurement, cryptocurrency for anonymous purchasing, and online forgery services are the primary acquisition channels for many capability types. Encrypted communications enable coordination with suppliers without generating conventional financial or communications records.

tactics.name: Network Development
tactics.notes: Recruiting and organizing facilitators, access agents, cutouts, and handlers who will support the operation. Network development converts a solo actor into a multi-node threat with distributed capabilities and deniability. For lone actors, this tactic may be absent — its absence is itself an indicator of actor type.
tactics.cpn_notes: Encrypted messaging apps, dark web forums, and privacy-preserving communications platforms are the coordination infrastructure for modern operational networks. Recruitment can occur entirely online with no physical meeting before execution.

tactics.name: Cover Construction
tactics.notes: Building a false identity or pretext that explains the actor's presence near the subject without raising suspicion. Cover construction ranges from a minimal behavioral pretext (posing as a jogger) to a full legend (fabricated identity with verifiable history).
tactics.cpn_notes: Synthetic social media presence, forged digital credentials, and fake email and phone infrastructure are standard components of modern cover identity construction. A digital footprint is now an expected component of a credible legend.

tactics.name: Pathway Indicators
tactics.notes: Observable pre-attack behavioral signals — grievance escalation, ideation, leakage, and preparation behaviors — that indicate an actor is moving toward violence. For fixated individuals and insider threats, pathway indicators are often the only pre-attack detection window. These are not tactics the actor chooses to execute; they are behavioral byproducts of the threat development process detectable by protective intelligence practitioners.
tactics.cpn_notes: Online leakage via social media posts, forum activity, messaging apps, and blog content is now the most detectable pre-attack indicator channel. Digital leakage often occurs before physical preparation begins and may continue throughout the threat development cycle.

tactics.name: Surveillance Detection Countermeasures
tactics.notes: Actor takes deliberate actions to detect whether they are under protective intelligence or law enforcement surveillance. The presence of this behavior signals operational sophistication and awareness of protective programs. Detection of counter-surveillance attempts is itself a high-confidence indicator of a developing threat.
tactics.cpn_notes: Digital counter-surveillance includes checking devices for surveillance software, monitoring social media for mentions of their name or image, and using RF detection apps to identify surveillance equipment. Sophisticated actors may monitor protective intelligence social media accounts for indicators of active investigation.

tactics.name: Operational Rehearsal
tactics.notes: Controlled dry run of the planned approach — timing assessments, route confirmation, access testing, go/no-go decision point validation. Operational rehearsal is a controlled exercise, not the live approach. Distinct from Partial Approach Rehearsal (TA0209), which uses the live target site with abort triggers.
tactics.cpn_notes: GPS and mapping apps, real-time traffic data, and satellite imagery are standard rehearsal planning tools. Digital simulation of timing and routing can substitute for physical rehearsal in some scenarios.

tactics.name: Concealment
tactics.notes: Managing physical appearance and behavior to avoid detection under human observation. Actor blends into the environment appropriate to the location and context — adjusting dress, behavior, pace, and attention pattern to match expected profiles. Concealment is an ongoing behavioral discipline throughout the Approach phase, not a single action.

tactics.name: Perimeter Breach
tactics.notes: Defeating the outermost security layer protecting access to the subject — building perimeters, residential security, vehicle protection details, or controlled access zones. The method varies by target protective posture: social engineering for low-profile targets, technical defeat for hardened environments.
tactics.cpn_notes: Credential cloning, RFID/NFC skimming, and electronic lock exploit tools are cyber-enabled defeat methods that bypass physical barriers without direct force. Access control systems that are digitally exploitable change the threat calculus for protected subjects in secured environments.

tactics.name: Zone Penetration
tactics.notes: Moving through successive security layers after initial perimeter breach — from public space to controlled access, from controlled access to restricted zones, from restricted zones to the subject's location. Each layer requires a distinct access method; failure at any layer aborts the approach.
tactics.cpn_notes: Electronic lock bypass and elevator system manipulation extend the actor's reach through security-tiered environments. Physical security systems with digital control planes are vulnerable to zone penetration via cyber methods without physical force.

tactics.name: Access Elevation
tactics.notes: Gaining higher-tier physical access through deception, social engineering, or credential abuse rather than force. Actor obtains access rights or physical proximity that their actual status does not authorize — vendor badge, escort manipulation, insider assistance. Distinct from Barrier Defeat (TA0302), which uses force.
tactics.cpn_notes: Cloned access credentials, spoofed caller ID, and fabricated digital authorization documents enable access elevation without physical contact. Attackers may use digitally-generated fake work orders or appointment confirmations to pass social engineering checkpoints.

tactics.name: Position Establishment
tactics.notes: Achieving and holding the physical position required to execute the planned action against the subject. Position establishment may be brief (a sniper position held for minutes) or sustained (a surveillance position maintained over days). The position is the final precondition before Phase 3 can begin.

tactics.name: Operational Pause
tactics.notes: Deliberate stand-down to allow counter-surveillance conditions to clear, security posture to relax, or an environmental opportunity to materialize. Operational pause is a risk management decision — the actor has the capability and position but chooses to wait. Generates cold trails that complicate later investigation.

tactics.name: Timing Exploitation
tactics.notes: Synchronizing the approach and execution with security shift changes, crowd events, delivery windows, environmental conditions, or protective detail gaps that create a predictable vulnerability window. Timing exploitation requires pattern knowledge from Subject Profiling and Environmental Survey.
tactics.cpn_notes: Live event feeds, venue capacity trackers, real-time traffic monitoring, and social media location tagging allow precise execution timing without physical pre-positioning. An actor can confirm the subject's real-time location and security context remotely before committing to approach.

tactics.name: Partial Approach Rehearsal
tactics.notes: Actor executes a partial approach sequence with pre-set abort triggers — approaching the subject's location, observing response, then withdrawing without completing the action. Repeated across multiple days or occasions before committing to full execution. Distinct from Operational Rehearsal (TA0201), which is a controlled dry run at a surrogate location. Partial Approach Rehearsal uses the live target environment and generates observable forensic signatures: access log anomalies, CCTV appearances near the subject's location, and witness sightings that establish a pre-attack timeline.

tactics.name: Force Application
tactics.notes: The deliberate use of violence, coercion, or seizure against the subject. The mechanism of harm. Sub-techniques capture the full range of force methods: lethal ranged attack, lethal contact attack, area or mass lethal attack, non-lethal incapacitation, abduction or seizure, coercive display, property destruction, and area denial. Force Application was the critical missing tactic in early framework versions — it is the apex behavior that all preceding phases build toward.

tactics.name: Barrier Defeat
tactics.notes: Physically defeating access controls or protective barriers through force — forced entry, vehicle ramming, physical overpowering of a bodyguard. Distinct from Access Elevation (TA0205), which uses deception. Barrier Defeat occurs when deception-based access has failed or when the actor chooses force as the primary method.

tactics.name: Objective Seizure
tactics.notes: Securing the operational target — the person, the information they carry, the asset they control, or the location they occupy. In kidnapping operations, Objective Seizure is the physical act of taking control of the subject. In intelligence operations, it may be the acquisition of a device or document the subject possesses.

tactics.name: Captive Control
tactics.notes: Controlling one or more persons during kidnapping, barricade, or hostage scenarios. Includes physical restraint, psychological management, and ongoing compliance maintenance. Captive Control is unique to the Person matrix — there is no structural analog in the other three matrices. The duration and quality of captive control directly determines the operational leverage in Phase 4.

tactics.name: Response Suppression
tactics.notes: Delaying or degrading the security or law enforcement response to extend the window for action on objective. Methods include communications jamming, diversionary incidents at alternate locations, access route blocking, and neutralizing security personnel. Response suppression is particularly critical in high-profile operations where a rapid LE response is expected.
tactics.cpn_notes: Radio frequency jamming blocks security communications. Spoofed emergency calls create diversionary response commitments. Cyber-enabled disabling of security systems — cameras, communications, access controls — can suppress response without physical presence at the response suppression point.

tactics.name: Bystander Management
tactics.notes: Controlling, dispersing, or neutralizing non-target individuals who are present during execution and who could interfere, report, or serve as witnesses. In person-targeted operations, bystander management ranges from verbal control of witnesses to physical neutralization of protective personnel. The presence of bystanders forces a tactical decision: neutralize, manage, or accept the exposure.

tactics.name: Counter-Surveillance
tactics.notes: Real-time environmental assessment during execution — continuously monitoring for law enforcement, protective detail response, witness attention, or camera coverage that would require the actor to abort or modify the action. Counter-surveillance during execution is distinct from pre-operational surveillance detection (TA0108), which occurs in Phase 1.
tactics.cpn_notes: Monitoring live camera feeds, checking police scanner apps, and using drone overwatch are cyber-enabled counter-surveillance methods increasingly used during execution. Real-time tracking of security force locations via their own digital signals is an emerging technique for sophisticated actors.

tactics.name: Operational Communication
tactics.notes: Real-time coordination among operation members during execution — signaling timing, position, role completion, and abort/continue decisions. For solo actors this tactic is absent. For multi-actor operations, operational communication is the nervous system of execution; its failure cascades across all other Phase 3 tactics.
tactics.cpn_notes: Encrypted communications apps, pre-arranged signal codes via SMS, and covert earpiece systems are standard operational communications infrastructure. Radio systems can be jammed by adversaries; encrypted data communications provide more resilient coordination.

tactics.name: Withdrawal
tactics.notes: Physical extraction from the operational area following the action on objective. Withdrawal is planned in Phase 1 as an integral component of the operation — not improvised after execution. Route, timing, cover, and transport method are all pre-selected. The window between action completion and security perimeter establishment is the critical variable.

tactics.name: Evidence Management
tactics.notes: Counter-forensics actions to reduce the traceability of the operation — physical evidence destruction, digital cleanup, biological material management, scene staging. The sophistication of evidence management is a reliable actor classification signal: lone actors rarely execute systematic counter-forensics; state actors plan it as a Phase 1 requirement.

tactics.name: Cover Reestablishment
tactics.notes: Returning to a normal behavioral envelope after the operation — reestablishing the appearance that the actor was not involved. Includes alibi establishment, contact normalization with colleagues and associates, and behavioral management to avoid post-incident detection.

tactics.name: Network Protection
tactics.notes: Protecting remaining members of the operational network — communication shutdown, personnel dispersal, infrastructure removal, and cutout management. For organized actors, network protection determines whether a single operation exposure becomes a network-wide compromise. For lone actors, this tactic is absent.

tactics.name: Financial Disposition
tactics.notes: Processing ransom payments, receiving contracted payment for professional actors, moving funds to obscure origin, and managing cryptocurrency associated with the operation. Financial Disposition is present when a financial motive exists — kidnap-for-ransom, contract operations, extortion.
tactics.cpn_notes: Cryptocurrency is the primary post-operation payment mechanism for both ransom and contract operations. Mixing services, chain hopping, and privacy coins are the standard obfuscation chain. Financial forensics on blockchain transactions have become a primary investigative tool — sophistication of financial disposition directly determines investigative success.

tactics.name: After-Action Assessment
tactics.notes: Adversary evaluation of what worked, what failed, and what must change for future operations. After-Action Assessment is present only for persistent, organized actors with intent to operate again. It closes the Operational Feedback Loop — the output of this tactic feeds directly into Phase 1 Target Development for the next operation. Lone actors and opportunity-based actors typically do not execute this tactic.

tactics.name: Attribution Declaration
tactics.notes: Actor claims responsibility for the operation — communiqué, propaganda release, manifesto delivery, media contact, or direct communication to authorities. Attribution declaration is the defining behavior of terrorists, ideologically motivated actors, and extortionists. The claim is the communicative act that converts physical violence into political or social leverage.

tactics.name: Audience Amplification
tactics.notes: Actor maximizes the reach and impact of the attribution message — media exploitation, social platform posting, staged documentation distribution, manipulation of coverage framing. Audience Amplification is the force multiplier for attribution-seeking actors: the operation's communicative impact is proportional to audience reach.
tactics.cpn_notes: Social media platforms, video hosting sites, and encrypted broadcast channels have displaced traditional media as the primary audience amplification channel. Actors with media production capabilities can release high-quality content that drives algorithmic amplification without press contact.

tactics.name: Coercive Leverage
tactics.notes: Actor makes direct contact with the victim, victim's family, or relevant decision-makers to assert leverage — ransom demand, negotiation, proof-of-life, threat escalation, and deadline management. Coercive Leverage requires the actor to have achieved a position of power (captive, stolen asset, credible threat) and now converts that position into a demand.

actor_profiles.name: Fixated Individual
actor_profiles.description: Psychologically-driven actor with obsessive focus on a specific named target. The fixation is the defining characteristic — this actor's threat is organized entirely around one person. Motivation is personal, delusional, or grievance-rooted. Follows a slow-building predatory pathway that may span months to years. Clinical anchor: WAVR-21, PATH model, Threat Assessment in Schools.
behavioral_markers: Sustained surveillance or contact attempts directed at one specific named target
Leakage of fixation content to third parties — friends, family, online platforms
Escalating attempts to communicate with or approach the target despite rejection
Acquisition of the target's personal information, schedule, and location patterns
Grievance documentation — letters, journals, social media posts referencing the target

actor_profiles.name: Domestic / Intimate Partner
actor_profiles.description: Actor in or formerly in an intimate, family, or cohabiting relationship with the target. The prior relationship is both the access vector and the motivational anchor. Distinct from the Fixated Individual — the pathway is rooted in control, jealousy, or retaliation rather than obsessive attachment from a distance. Covers intimate partner violence, domestic homicide, stalking by former partners, and family-based violence.
behavioral_markers: Prior or current intimate/family relationship with the target
History of controlling, coercive, or threatening behavior within the relationship
Violation of protective or restraining orders
Monitoring of target's location, communications, and social relationships
Escalation following separation, divorce proceedings, or custody disputes

actor_profiles.name: Crisis-Driven
actor_profiles.description: Individual actor whose pathway to violence is precipitated by an identifiable personal crisis — job loss, financial ruin, relationship breakdown, mental health episode, or a desire for notoriety and legacy. Unlike the Fixated Individual, the target is often symbolic or opportunistic rather than a specific named person. The crisis compresses Phase 1 dramatically: the window from ideation to action may be days or weeks, not months. The notoriety-seeking dimension connects this actor type to attribution-seeking Phase 4 behavior even without ideological content.
behavioral_markers: Identifiable precipitating crisis event immediately preceding behavioral change
Rapid deterioration in work performance, relationships, or personal stability
Leakage of intent — often to multiple people, sometimes publicly
Language expressing desire to be known, remembered, or understood
Target selection based on symbolic representation of the grievance source, not personal relationship

actor_profiles.name: Malicious Insider
actor_profiles.description: Current employee, contractor, or trusted-access holder who deliberately exploits their legitimate access to harm the organization, its people, or its assets. The trust relationship is the attack surface. Motivation may be grievance-based, financially motivated, or ideological. Phase 1 is compressed because the actor is already inside — they know the target, the layout, the security gaps, and the personnel.
behavioral_markers: Observable behavioral deterioration — withdrawal, hostility, policy violations
Anomalous access patterns — systems, locations, or data outside their operational need
Unexplained external contact establishment or suspicious external relationships
Acquisition of materials inconsistent with job role (portable storage, recording equipment)
Testing organizational response to minor violations to calibrate detection capability

actor_profiles.name: Coerced Insider
actor_profiles.description: Current trusted-access holder who is being blackmailed, threatened, or otherwise coerced into facilitating an attack or intelligence collection operation. The actor knows what they are doing but is not acting freely. Coercion may be based on personal compromising information, threats to family, or financial leverage. Detection is complicated because the actor's outward grievance indicators are suppressed — they appear compliant rather than hostile.
behavioral_markers: Sudden behavioral change without apparent internal grievance — anxiety, hypervigilance, social withdrawal
Unexplained financial pressure or sudden resolution of financial problems
Unusual communications security behaviors — new devices, encrypted apps, changed routines
Compliance with unusual external requests without pushback or documentation
Reluctance to discuss personal life; possible indicators of external surveillance of the actor themselves

actor_profiles.name: Recruited Insider
actor_profiles.description: Current trusted-access holder who has been deliberately recruited by an external actor — typically a state intelligence service, organized criminal group, or competitor — and is willingly cooperating. Unlike the coerced insider, this actor chose the relationship. They are disciplined, operationally patient, and actively work to expand their access and preserve their position. Among the hardest insider threat profiles to detect.
behavioral_markers: Sustained covert relationship with an external handler, often disguised as a professional or personal contact
Deliberate access scope expansion beyond their operational role over time
Disciplined information aggregation across systems and people without triggering individual alarms
Unexplained affluence or financial activity inconsistent with salary
High operational security — carefully manages evidence trail, may generate false audit records

actor_profiles.name: Negligent Insider
actor_profiles.description: Current trusted-access holder who creates risk through carelessness, poor security hygiene, or disregard for policy — without intent to cause harm. Not a threat actor in the traditional sense, but a significant risk vector. The negligent insider is frequently the entry point exploited by external actors. Susceptibility to social engineering and phishing is the primary mechanism.
behavioral_markers: Repeated security policy violations without apparent concern for consequences
Susceptibility to social engineering attempts — shares passwords, follows unauthorized instructions
Ignores escalating warning signals or security notifications
Uses personal devices or unsecured channels for sensitive work
Does not report suspicious contacts or requests from unknown parties

actor_profiles.name: Unwitting Insider
actor_profiles.description: Current trusted-access holder who is being exploited by an external actor without their knowledge. Their normal work behavior is the delivery mechanism. They may provide access, information, or physical proximity that they believe is routine. Detection requires identifying the external actor doing the exploiting — the insider's behavior appears normal because it is normal from their perspective. A state intelligence service using a foreign business contact as an unwitting collection platform is the archetype.
behavioral_markers: From the insider's perspective: normal behavior — no behavioral indicators present
Detection pathway: identify the external actor, then map who they have access to
May be approached under a professional or personal pretext with no indication of intelligence purpose
The exploiting actor manages the relationship to keep the insider unaware
Post-detection: insider is typically a victim/witness, not a prosecutorial target

actor_profiles.name: Opportunistic Criminal
actor_profiles.description: Financially motivated actor with minimal planning who exploits available opportunity. Target is selected based on perceived vulnerability, not advance research. Low operational sophistication and low operational security — evidence trail is typically recoverable. Violence is instrumental and proportional to perceived resistance. Acts quickly on available conditions rather than waiting for optimal timing.
behavioral_markers: Target selection based on visible vulnerability — unlocked vehicle, isolated pedestrian, unattended property
Minimal pre-attack preparation or reconnaissance
Low operational security — no counter-surveillance behavior
Improvised capability — uses whatever is at hand
Pattern of escalation from minor offenses toward more serious crimes

actor_profiles.name: Professional Criminal
actor_profiles.description: Financially motivated actor operating as a hired professional — assassin, kidnap-for-ransom specialist, security contractor operating outside legal bounds. Executes for payment, not ideology. Applies professional tradecraft: pre-operational surveillance, covert capability acquisition, practiced withdrawal. Full phase completion is the operational norm. Distinguishable from organized criminal enterprise by individual or small-team structure and contract-based operation.
behavioral_markers: Pre-operational surveillance of target using professional counter-detection methods
Covert capability acquisition through channels that obscure the procurement trail
No ideological or personal connection to the target — purely transactional
Systematic withdrawal and evidence management post-operation
Financial transaction pattern consistent with contracted payment receipt

actor_profiles.name: Organized Criminal Group
actor_profiles.description: Enterprise-level criminal organization with division of roles, financial infrastructure, and operational capacity spanning multiple operations. Cartel, syndicate, or organized crime network. Kidnap-for-ransom, extortion, trafficking, and targeted violence against rivals or witnesses are primary THREAT-relevant operations. Uses cutouts, intermediaries, and financial layering. Phase 4 Financial Disposition is a defining operational capability.
behavioral_markers: Multiple actors with compartmented and specialized roles
Financial infrastructure for receiving and laundering proceeds
Use of cutouts and intermediaries to separate principals from operations
Established communications security and counter-surveillance protocols
Pattern of prior incidents establishing organizational TTPs

actor_profiles.name: Corporate Spy
actor_profiles.description: Actor focused on covert intelligence collection — stealing trade secrets, IP, client data, strategic plans, or personnel information for delivery to a competitor, state actor, or criminal purchaser. The defining operational logic is preservation of access: unlike the saboteur, the corporate spy wants to keep going back. Stealth and access maintenance are primary operational values. May be an insider acting on behalf of an external principal or an external actor who has gained insider access through recruitment or social engineering.
behavioral_markers: Covert access to sensitive data or systems beyond their operational role
Aggregation of information across multiple systems and people in a pattern inconsistent with job function
Deliberate concealment of information collection activities — after-hours access, personal devices
Sustained contact with an external handler disguised as professional or personal relationship
Deliberate preservation of access — avoids triggering reviews, maintains low profile

actor_profiles.name: Industrial Saboteur
actor_profiles.description: Actor focused on physically destroying or disabling operational capability — machinery, production systems, infrastructure, or records. Unlike the corporate spy, the saboteur's operation is consumptive: the objective is to break something, not take something. May be grievance-driven, state-directed, competitor-directed, or ideologically motivated. Timing around business-critical events (product launch, merger, regulatory deadline) is a characteristic indicator. Uses legitimate access to reach systems that would otherwise require forced entry.
behavioral_markers: Access to operational or physical systems beyond normal job function
Unexplained presence near critical equipment, infrastructure nodes, or safety systems
Evidence of tampering with machinery, processes, or safety controls
Timing correlation with business-critical events or organizational stress periods
May disguise access as maintenance, inspection, or contractor activity

actor_profiles.name: Issue-Oriented Activist
actor_profiles.description: Ideologically motivated actor whose goal is exposure, embarrassment, disruption, or policy change — not physical harm to people. Physical harm to persons is actively avoided because it would undermine the message. Property damage and operational disruption are within scope. Attribution is essential — the claim is the point. Operates in a legal-to-illegal spectrum that makes early intervention difficult. Distinguishable from terrorist by the absence of mass casualty intent and the communicative primacy of the non-violent message.
behavioral_markers: Public organizing activity, social media coordination, and recruitment visible before the action
Targets selected for symbolic or operational significance to the cause, not for maximum casualties
Prior incidents of civil disobedience or low-level property disruption in the pattern
Strong attribution-seeking behavior — prepared statements, media contacts, livestreaming
Avoidance of methods likely to cause human casualties

actor_profiles.name: Saboteur
actor_profiles.description: Ideologically motivated actor whose goal is the destruction or disabling of infrastructure or operational capability. Physical harm to people is acceptable, not the primary objective. Targets are selected for their operational significance — pipelines, power nodes, water systems, communications infrastructure. May operate as a lone actor or in a small cell. Attribution is conditional: environmental or anti-state saboteurs may claim credit; state-affiliated saboteurs typically do not.
behavioral_markers: Reconnaissance of infrastructure targets — substations, pipelines, pumping stations, communication towers
Acquisition of destructive materials — explosives, incendiaries, or tools for physical tampering
Operational security behaviors suggesting awareness of surveillance
Target selection pattern focused on operational chokepoints and high-consequence nodes
May conduct Partial Approach Rehearsal at target sites across multiple days

actor_profiles.name: Terrorist
actor_profiles.description: Ideologically or politically motivated actor who uses violence as a communicative instrument — to coerce policy, shape public perception, or demonstrate capability. The violence is not the end; it is the message. Attribution is operationally required: terrorism without a claim loses its coercive power. Target selection is based on symbolic value or policy leverage, not maximum casualty efficiency. Organized cells apply full phase completion. Lone terrorists may compress Phase 1 based on radicalization timeline.
behavioral_markers: Ideological radicalization indicators — consumption of extremist content, rhetoric escalation, community withdrawal
Operational security behaviors — encrypted communications, new devices, changed routines
Target selection research focused on symbolic value or media visibility
Capability acquisition for mass-casualty or high-impact methods
Phase 4 attribution is mandatory — prepared communiqués, media contacts, or symbolic claim delivery

actor_profiles.name: Violent Extremist
actor_profiles.description: Ideologically motivated actor for whom causing mass harm or death is the operational end, not merely the instrument. Distinct from the terrorist — coercion is not the goal; elimination or destruction of the target group is. Attribution may be claimed or implicit but is not operationally required. Mass casualty capacity is the capability selection criterion. May exhibit martyrdom posture in Phase 4 — no evasion planning, active engagement with responders, or suicide by cop.
behavioral_markers: Escalating dehumanization of a target group in communications and expressed ideology
Leakage of eliminationist language — destruction, annihilation, cleansing rhetoric
Acquisition of mass-casualty capable methods — firearms, explosives, vehicles
Target selection based on demographic density of the target group, not symbolic value
Martyrdom posture — no withdrawal planning, final communications framed as legacy

actor_profiles.name: State Actor
actor_profiles.description: Nation-state directed, sponsored, or sanctioned adversary operating with the full intelligence apparatus of a state — collection, planning support, logistics, and exfiltration infrastructure. Deniability architecture is a Phase 1 planning input, not an afterthought. Highest capability across all matrices. May use proxy actors. Operations span all four matrices simultaneously for complex targets. Full phase completion is the norm; Phase 1 may extend over months with parallel Phase 2 preparation.
behavioral_markers: Professional counter-surveillance and surveillance detection routines throughout all phases
Multi-actor team with compartmented roles and need-to-know discipline
Access to sophisticated capability — specialized tools, forged documents, sterile communications
Pre-planned withdrawal with assets positioned before operation initiation
Deniability architecture visible in how the operation is structured and what is left behind

actor_profiles.name: State-Sponsored Actor
actor_profiles.description: Actor funded, directed, or enabled by a state intelligence service while maintaining a non-state appearance — contractor, criminal organization, hacktivist front, or private intelligence firm operating with state backing. Witting of the state relationship. The sponsoring state gains operational capability with plausible deniability. Distinct from State Actor (direct operation) — the sponsoring relationship adds a layer that complicates attribution and legal accountability.
behavioral_markers: Operates with resources and capability inconsistent with apparent organizational size or revenue
Access to state-grade intelligence products and tradecraft without apparent state affiliation
Operations align with sponsoring state's geopolitical interests or target lists
Financial flows from obscured state-affiliated sources
Counter-attribution behaviors designed to point to non-state actors

actor_profiles.name: Unwitting State Proxy
actor_profiles.description: An individual or organization exploited by a state intelligence service as an access or collection vehicle without their knowledge. Recruited under a false pretext — as a researcher, business contact, journalist, or academic — and used to provide physical access, information, or relationships that the state actor cannot reach directly. From the proxy's perspective, the relationship is entirely normal. Detection requires identifying the state handler and mapping who they have cultivated access to. The proxy is typically a victim, not a prosecutorial target.
behavioral_markers: From the proxy's perspective: entirely normal behavior — no meaningful behavioral indicators
Detection pathway: identify the state handler's relationship network, not the proxy's behavior
May have been approached with flattery, professional opportunity, or apparent friendship
Provides access or information to the handler believing it serves a legitimate purpose
Relationship managed by the handler to maintain the proxy's unawareness and continued cooperation

actor_profiles.name: State-Directed Corporate Front
actor_profiles.description: A commercial entity — business, research institution, joint venture, or consultancy — that operates as a vehicle for state intelligence collection or influence operations. Leadership is witting; operational staff may range from witting to entirely unwitting. The commercial cover provides access to sensitive environments that direct state actors cannot reach. Detection requires analysis of the corporate structure, beneficial ownership, and the pattern of access sought rather than behavioral indicators in individual personnel.
behavioral_markers: Corporate structure with beneficial ownership obscured through multiple jurisdictions
Business activities that create access to sensitive technology, infrastructure, or personnel
Employees who seek unusually broad access beyond their stated commercial purpose
Partnerships or contracts that provide physical or network access to protected environments
Pattern of interest in sensitive information inconsistent with stated commercial objectives

actor_profiles.name: Transnational Criminal Organization
actor_profiles.description: Formal criminal organization operating across national borders with quasi-state capabilities. Conducts kidnapping-for-ransom, assassination, extortion, smuggling, and corruption of government officials at scale. Includes cartels, transnational gangs, and criminal networks with documented convergence with state and terrorist actors. Characterized by territorial control, paramilitary units, and sophisticated operational security.
behavioral_markers: Surveillance and pattern-of-life collection on target consistent with professional operational planning
Use of cutouts, intermediaries, and encrypted communications to insulate principals
Multiple operational actors with defined roles — intelligence, logistics, execution
Cross-border financial flows and money laundering infrastructure
Willingness to conduct violence against witnesses, family members, or security personnel

actor_profiles.name: Contract Killer
actor_profiles.description: Individual engaged to commit murder in exchange for payment. May be employed by organized criminal groups, transnational criminal organizations, state actors, commercial actors, or private individuals. Professional execution and operational security distinguish this actor from impulsive criminal violence. The transactional nature means the actor has no personal grievance — threat does not de-escalate if the underlying grievance is resolved.
behavioral_markers: Methodical pre-attack surveillance and target confirmation behaviors
Use of counter-surveillance techniques and route variation during reconnaissance
Acquisition of weapons through channels designed to prevent attribution
Limited digital footprint and use of cash or cryptocurrency for operational expenses
No observable personal grievance with the target — access to target is the only objective

actor_profiles.name: Abductor
actor_profiles.description: Actor who takes a person by force, deception, or coercion for purposes of ransom, coercion, exploitation, or use as leverage against a third party. May operate independently or as a member of an organized group. Kidnapping-for-ransom operations involve sustained engagement with victim and family or organization over time. Coercive abduction may be used to extract access credentials, information, or compliance from the victim or a connected party.
behavioral_markers: Extended pattern-of-life collection to identify predictable routines and low-coverage windows
Acquisition of restraints, vehicles, or communications equipment inconsistent with known activities
Probing of security measures — testing response times, guard schedules, or escort procedures
Pre-positioning of resources at likely intercept points along target's regular routes
Establishment of secure holding location prior to execution

actor_profiles.name: Extortionist
actor_profiles.description: Actor who uses threatened harm — to a person, their family, their assets, or their reputation — to compel payment or behavioral compliance. May leverage compromising information, physical threats, or harm to third parties as leverage. Distinct from kidnapping in that the primary tool is the threat rather than the act. Threat may be sustained over extended periods and escalated incrementally to maximize compliance.
behavioral_markers: Initial contact establishing the threat and demand, often through anonymous or obfuscated channels
Demonstrated access to private information or ability to harm as proof of capability
Escalating contact frequency or threatened consequence when demands are not met
Monitoring of target's public communications and security posture for signs of law enforcement contact
Use of cryptocurrency or structured financial channels to receive payment without attribution

actor_profiles.name: Service User
actor_profiles.description: Individual who has a legitimate service relationship with the target organization — patient, client, student, customer — and directs violence or threatening behavior toward staff or other service users within that context. The highest-volume category of workplace violence in the United States (Cal/OSHA Type II). Threat typically emerges from dissatisfaction with service, denial of benefits, or perceived mistreatment. Violence is often impulsive and situationally triggered rather than premeditated.
behavioral_markers: Verbal aggression or threatening statements directed at staff during service interactions
Escalating complaints with explicit or implicit threats of harm
History of disruptive behavior across multiple service interactions
Expressed grievance specifically targeting a named staff member or service location
Presence in restricted areas or repeated attempts to access staff outside normal service channels

actor_profiles.name: Aggrieved Group
actor_profiles.description: Organized or semi-organized collective with a shared grievance against a specific organization, its leadership, or its policies. Distinguished from ideological actors (AP015–AP018) by the specificity and personal nature of the grievance — this group was directly affected by a decision, product failure, policy, or perceived injustice. May organize protests, harassment campaigns, coordinated complaints, or escalate to physical disruption and targeted threats against executives or staff.
behavioral_markers: Coordinated contact campaigns — calls, emails, social media — directed at specific staff or leadership
Organized protest activity at organizational facilities or events
Doxing of executive or staff personal information to group members
Explicit threatening communications directed at named individuals
Escalation pattern: public campaign → targeted harassment → physical threat

warden_integration.notes: WARDEN alert schema maps to THREAT taxonomy via agentic ingestion pipeline. V3 implementation: WARDEN behavioral alerts → structured TTP assessment in THREAT taxonomy language. Both tools are demonstrably more powerful together — WARDEN detects the pathway; THREAT maps where the actor is in the kill chain.

ai_architecture.philosophy: AI is a force multiplier on existing attack vectors — not a separate pathway. AI compresses Phase 1 timelines, lowers tradecraft requirements, and makes capabilities previously requiring nation-state resources accessible to unsophisticated actors. It does not create new physical attack pathways; it reduces the skill floor for existing ones.

vectors.ai_initiated_physical: An autonomous or semi-autonomous AI system directly executes physical harm without real-time human direction at the point of attack. Distinct from cyber_initiated_physical: the attack does not necessarily route through networked cyberspace — the AI agent operates locally, autonomously, in physical space. Examples: AI-directed drones with autonomous targeting, robotic platforms executing without human oversight, compromised autonomous vehicle systems.
vectors.ai_enabled_risks: Capability modifier field on every actor profile. Documents AI-specific risk amplifiers applicable to each actor type. Not a vector — an inventory of how AI tools lower the barrier for existing attack vectors on a per-profile basis.

capability_taxonomy.accelerated_target_research: LLMs, OSINT aggregators, and AI-powered social graph analyzers compress Phase 1 target development from weeks to hours, without requiring technical expertise.
capability_taxonomy.synthetic_identity: AI-generated personas, documents, voice, and online presence used for cover identities, social engineering, or recruitment operations.
capability_taxonomy.deepfake_manipulation: AI-generated audio, video, or text impersonating trusted parties — used for manipulation, extortion, or access enablement.
capability_taxonomy.automated_surveillance: AI-powered monitoring of a target's digital footprint, location patterns, social connections, and behavioral indicators at scale.
capability_taxonomy.autonomous_targeting: AI systems capable of independent targeting decisions for physical harm, without real-time human direction at point of execution.

ai_architecture.cpn_impact: The Cyber-Physical Nexus dimension carries the highest AI amplification risk. AI tools directly accelerate Phase 1 (Target Development) for any actor targeting individuals with a digital footprint. Detection models must account for AI-compressed threat timelines — a Fixated Individual now has access to research capability that previously required professional-grade intelligence resources.
ai_architecture.v2_roadmap: V2 will add per-tactic AI capability annotations, expand ai_enabled_risks with tactic-level associations, and introduce the Operations/scenario composer with AI-assisted behavioral pattern generation.
